modules.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc
documentation:
Msgpack-rpc subpackage.

This package implements a msgpack-rpc client. While it was designed for
handling some Nvim particularities(server->client requests for example), the
code here should work with other msgpack-rpc servers.

functions.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.tcp_session
documentation: Create a msgpack-rpc session from a tcp address/port.

functions.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.socket_session
documentation: Create a msgpack-rpc session from a unix domain socket.

functions.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.stdio_session
documentation: Create a msgpack-rpc session from stdin/stdout.

functions.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.child_session
documentation: Create a msgpack-rpc session from a new Nvim instance.

modules.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session
documentation: Asynchronous msgpack-rpc handling in the event loop pipeline.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.__init__
documentation: Wrap `msgpack_stream` on a msgpack-rpc interface.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.threadsafe_call
documentation: Wrapper around `MsgpackStream.threadsafe_call`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.request
documentation:
Send a msgpack-rpc request to Nvim.

A msgpack-rpc with method `method` and argument `args` is sent to
Nvim. The `response_cb` function is called with when the response
is available.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.notify
documentation:
Send a msgpack-rpc notification to Nvim.

A msgpack-rpc with method `method` and argument `args` is sent to
Nvim. This will have the same effect as a request, but no response
will be received

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.run
documentation:
Run the event loop to receive requests and notifications from Nvim.

While the event loop is running, `request_cb` and `notification_cb`
will be called whenever requests or notifications are respectively
available.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.stop
documentation: Stop the event loop.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.AsyncSession.close
documentation: Close the event loop.

Asynchronous msgpack-rpc layer that wraps a msgpack stream.

This wraps the msgpack stream interface for reading/writing msgpack
documents and exposes an interface for sending and receiving msgpack-rpc
requests and notifications.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.Response

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.Response.__init__
documentation: Initialize the Response instance.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.async_session.Response.send
documentation:
Send the response.

If `error` is True, it will be sent as an error.

Response to a msgpack-rpc request that came from Nvim.

When Nvim sends a msgpack-rpc request, an instance of this class is
created for remembering state required to send a response.

documentation: Msgpack handling in the event loop pipeline.

documentation: Wrap `event_loop` on a msgpack-aware interface.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.msgpack_stream.MsgpackStream.threadsafe_call
documentation: Wrapper around `BaseEventLoop.threadsafe_call`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.msgpack_stream.MsgpackStream.send
documentation: Queue `msg` for sending to Nvim.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.msgpack_stream.MsgpackStream.run
documentation:
Run the event loop to receive messages from Nvim.

While the event loop is running, `message_cb` will be called whenever
a message has been successfully parsed from the input stream.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.msgpack_stream.MsgpackStream.stop
documentation: Stop the event loop.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.msgpack_stream.MsgpackStream.close
documentation: Close the event loop.

Two-way msgpack stream that wraps a event loop byte stream.

This wraps the event loop interface for reading/writing bytes and
exposes an interface for reading/writing msgpack documents.

modules.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop
documentation:
Event loop abstraction subpackage.

We use python's built-in asyncio as the backend.

modules.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session
documentation: Synchronous msgpack-rpc session layer.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Request
documentation: A request from Nvim.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Notification
documentation: A notification from Nvim.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.__init__
documentation: Wrap `async_session` on a synchronous msgpack-rpc interface.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.loop
documentation: Get the underlying msgpack EventLoop.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.threadsafe_call
documentation: Wrapper around `AsyncSession.threadsafe_call`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.next_message
documentation:
Block until a message(request or notification) is available.

If any messages were previously enqueued, return the first in queue.
If not, run the event loop until one is received.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.request
documentation:
Send a msgpack-rpc request and block until as response is received.

If the event loop is running, this method must have been called by a
request or notification handler running on a greenlet. In that case,
send the quest and yield to the parent greenlet until a response is
available.

When the event loop is not running, it will perform a blocking request
like this:
- Send the request
- Run the loop until the response is available
- Put requests/notifications received while waiting into a queue

If the `async_` flag is present and True, a asynchronous notification
is sent instead. This will never block, and the return value or error
is ignored.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.run
documentation:
Run the event loop to receive requests and notifications from Nvim.

Like `AsyncSession.run()`, but `request_cb` and `notification_cb` are
inside greenlets.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.stop
documentation: Stop the event loop.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.Session.close
documentation: Close the event loop.

Msgpack-rpc session layer that uses coroutines for a synchronous API.

This class provides the public msgpack-rpc API required by this library.
It uses the greenlet module to handle requests and notifications coming
from Nvim with a synchronous API.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.session.ErrorResponse
documentation:
Raise this in a request handler to respond with a given error message.

Unlike when other exceptions are caught, this gives full control off the
error response sent. When "ErrorResponse(msg)" is caught "msg" will be
sent verbatim as the error response.No traceback will be appended.

modules.name: pynvim.plugin
documentation: Nvim plugin/host subpackage.

modules.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base
documentation: Common code for event loop implementations.

classes.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.__init__
documentation:
Initialize and connect the event loop instance.

The only arguments are the transport type and transport-specific
configuration, like this:

```pycon
>>> BaseEventLoop('tcp', '127.0.0.1', 7450)
>>> BaseEventLoop('socket', '/tmp/nvim-socket')
>>> BaseEventLoop('stdio')
>>> BaseEventLoop('child', ['nvim', '--embed', '--headless', '-u', 'NONE'])
```

Implementation-specific initialization should be made in the \_\_init\_\_
constructor of the subclass, which must call the constructor of the
super class (BaseEventLoop), in which one of the `_connect_*` methods
(based on `transport_type`) and then `_start_reading()`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.connect_tcp
documentation: Connect to tcp/ip `address`:`port`. Delegated to `_connect_tcp`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.connect_socket
documentation: Connect to socket at `path`. Delegated to `_connect_socket`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.connect_stdio
documentation: Connect using stdin/stdout. Delegated to `_connect_stdio`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.connect_child
documentation: Connect a new Nvim instance. Delegated to `_connect_child`.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.send
documentation: Queue `data` for sending to Nvim.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.threadsafe_call
documentation:
Call a function in the event loop thread.

This is the only safe way to interact with a session from other
threads.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.run
documentation: Run the event loop, and receives response messages to a callback.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.stop
documentation: Stop the event loop.

methods.name: pynvim.msgpack_rpc.event_loop.base.BaseEventLoop.close
documentation: Stop the event loop.

Abstract base class for all event loops.

Event loops act as the bottom layer for Nvim sessions created by this
library. They hide system/transport details behind a simple interface for
reading/writing bytes to the connected Nvim instance.

A lifecycle of event loop is as follows: (1. -> [2. -> 3.]* -> 4.)
  1. initialization (__init__): connection to Nvim is established.
  2. run(data_cb): run the event loop (blocks until the loop stops).
     Requests are sent to the remote neovim by calling send(), and
     responses (messages) from the remote neovim will be passed to the
     given `data_cb` callback function while the event loop is running.
     Note that run() may be called multiple times.
  3. stop(): stop the event loop.
  4. close(): close the event loop, destroying all the internal resources.

This class exposes public methods for interacting with the underlying
event loop and delegates implementation-specific work to the following
methods, which subclasses are expected to implement:

- `_init()`: Implementation-specific initialization
- `_connect_tcp(address, port)`: connect to Nvim using tcp/ip
- `_connect_socket(path)`: Same as tcp, but use a UNIX domain socket or
  named pipe.
- `_connect_stdio()`: Use stdin/stdout as the connection to Nvim
- `_connect_child(argv)`: Use the argument vector `argv` to spawn an
  embedded Nvim that has its stdin/stdout connected to the event loop.
- `_start_reading()`: Called after any of _connect_* methods. Can be used
  to perform any post-connection setup or validation.
- `_send(data)`: Send `data` (byte array) to Nvim (usually RPC request).
- `_run()`: Runs the event loop until stopped or the connection is closed.
  The following methods can be called upon some events by the event loop:
  - `_on_data(data)`: When Nvim sends some data (usually RPC response).
  - `_on_signal(signum)`: When a signal is received.
  - `_on_error(exc)`: When a non-recoverable error occurs (e.g:
    connection lost, or any other OSError)
  Note that these _on_{data,signal,error} methods are not 'final', may be
  changed around an execution of run(). The subclasses are expected to
  handle any early messages arriving while _on_data is not yet set.
- `_stop()`: Stop the event loop.
- `_interrupt(data)`: Like `stop()`, but may be called from other threads
  this.
- `_setup_signals(signals)`: Add implementation-specific listeners for
  for `signals`, which is a list of OS-specific signal numbers.
- `_teardown_signals()`: Removes signal listeners set by `_setup_signals`
TODO: Since pynvim now supports python 3, the only available backend of the
msgpack_rpc BaseEventLoop is the built-in asyncio (see #294). We will have
to remove some unnecessary abstractions as well as greenlet. See also #489

modules.name: pynvim
documentation:
Python client for Nvim.

Client library for talking with Nvim processes via its msgpack-rpc API.

functions.name: pynvim.start_host
documentation:
Promote the current process into python plugin host for Nvim.

Start msgpack-rpc event loop for `session`, listening for Nvim requests
and notifications. It registers Nvim commands for loading/unloading
python plugins.

The sys.stdout and sys.stderr streams are redirected to Nvim through
`session`. That means print statements probably won't work as expected
while this function doesn't return.

This function is normally called at program startup and could have been
defined as a separate executable. It is exposed as a library function for
testing purposes only.

functions.name: pynvim.attach
documentation:
Provide a nicer interface to create python api sessions.

Previous machinery to create python api sessions is still there. This only
creates a facade function to make things easier for the most usual cases.
Thus, instead of:
    from pynvim import socket_session, Nvim
    session = tcp_session(address=<address>, port=<port>)
    nvim = Nvim.from_session(session)
You can now do:
    from pynvim import attach
    nvim = attach('tcp', address=<address>, port=<port>)
And also:
    nvim = attach('socket', path=<path>)
    nvim = attach('child', argv=<argv>)
    nvim = attach('stdio')

When the session is not needed anymore, it is recommended to explicitly
close it:
   nvim.close()
It is also possible to use the session as a context manager:
   with attach('socket', path=thepath) as nvim:
       print(nvim.funcs.getpid())
       print(nvim.current.line)
This will automatically close the session when you're done with it, or
when an error occurred.

Represents a string->object map stored in Nvim.

This is the dict counterpart to the `RemoteSequence` class, but it is used
as a generic way of retrieving values from the various map-like data
structures present in Nvim.

It is used to provide a dict-like API to vim variables and options.

classes.name: pynvim.api.common.RemoteSequence

methods.name: pynvim.api.common.RemoteSequence.__init__
documentation: Initialize a RemoteSequence with session, method.

documentation: Return the length of the remote sequence.

documentation: Return a sequence item by index.

methods.name: pynvim.api.common.RemoteSequence.__iter__
documentation: Return an iterator for the sequence.

methods.name: pynvim.api.common.RemoteSequence.__contains__
documentation: Check if an item is present in the sequence.

Represents a sequence of objects stored in Nvim.

This class is used to wrap msgpack-rpc functions that work on Nvim
sequences(of lines, buffers, windows and tabpages) with an API that
is similar to the one provided by the python-vim interface.

For example, the 'windows' property of the `Nvim` class is a RemoteSequence
sequence instance, and the expression `nvim.windows[0]` is translated to
session.request('nvim_list_wins')[0].

One important detail about this class is that all methods will fetch the
sequence into a list and perform the necessary manipulation
locally(iteration, indexing, counting, etc).

modules.name: pynvim.api.buffer
documentation: API for working with a Nvim Buffer.

functions.name: pynvim.api.buffer.adjust_index
documentation: Convert from python indexing convention to nvim indexing convention.

classes.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__init__
documentation: Initialize from Nvim and code_data immutable object.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__len__
documentation: Return the number of lines contained in a Buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__getitem__
documentation:
Get a buffer line or slice by integer index.

Indexes may be negative to specify positions from the end of the
buffer. For example, -1 is the last line, -2 is the line before that
and so on.

When retrieving slices, omitting indexes(eg: `buffer[:]`) will bring
the whole buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__setitem__
documentation:
Replace a buffer line or slice by integer index.

Like with `__getitem__`, indexes may be negative.

When replacing slices, omitting indexes(eg: `buffer[:]`) will replace
the whole buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__iter__
documentation:
Iterate lines of a buffer.

This will retrieve all lines locally before iteration starts. This
approach is used because for most cases, the gain is much greater by
minimizing the number of API calls by transferring all data needed to
work.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__delitem__
documentation:
Delete line or slice of lines from the buffer.

This is the same as __setitem__(idx, [])

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.__ne__
documentation:
Test inequality of Buffers.

Necessary for Python 2 compatibility.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.append
documentation: Append a string or list of lines to the buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.mark
documentation: Return (row, col) tuple for a named mark.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.range
documentation: Return a `Range` object, which represents part of the Buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.add_highlight
documentation: Add a highlight to the buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.clear_highlight
documentation: Clear highlights from the buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.update_highlights
documentation:
Add or update highlights in batch to avoid unnecessary redraws.

A `src_id` must have been allocated prior to use of this function. Use
for instance `nvim.new_highlight_source()` to get a src_id for your
plugin.

`hls` should be a list of highlight items. Each item should be a list
or tuple on the form `("GroupName", linenr, col_start, col_end)` or
`("GroupName", linenr)` to highlight an entire line.

By default existing highlights are preserved. Specify a line range with
clear_start and clear_end to replace highlights in this range. As a
shorthand, use clear=True to clear the entire buffer before adding the
new highlights.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.name
documentation: Get the buffer name.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.name
documentation: Set the buffer name. BufFilePre/BufFilePost are triggered.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.valid
documentation: Return True if the buffer still exists.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.loaded
documentation: Return True if the buffer is valid and loaded.

methods.name: pynvim.api.buffer.Buffer.number
documentation: Get the buffer number.
A remote Nvim buffer.

modules.name: pynvim.api.nvim
documentation: Main Nvim interface.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.from_session
documentation:
Create a new Nvim instance for a Session instance.

This method must be called to create the first Nvim instance, since it
queries Nvim metadata for type information and sets a SessionHook for
creating specialized objects from Nvim remote handles.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.from_nvim
documentation: Create a new Nvim instance from an existing instance.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.__init__
documentation: Initialize a new Nvim instance. This method is module-private.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.loop
documentation: Get the event loop (exposed to rplugins).

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.request
documentation:
Send an API request or notification to nvim.

It is rarely needed to call this function directly, as most API
functions have python wrapper functions. The `api` object can
be also be used to call API functions as methods:

    vim.api.err_write('ERROR\n', async_=True)
    vim.current.buffer.api.get_mark('.')

is equivalent to

    vim.request('nvim_err_write', 'ERROR\n', async_=True)
    vim.request('nvim_buf_get_mark', vim.current.buffer, '.')


Normally a blocking request will be sent.  If the `async_` flag is
present and True, a asynchronous notification is sent instead. This
will never block, and the return value or error is ignored.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.next_message
documentation:
Block until a message(request or notification) is available.

If any messages were previously enqueued, return the first in queue.
If not, run the event loop until one is received.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.run_loop
documentation:
Run the event loop to receive requests and notifications from Nvim.

This should not be called from a plugin running in the host, which
already runs the loop and dispatches events to plugins.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.stop_loop
documentation: Stop the event loop being started with `run_loop`.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.close
documentation: Close the nvim session and release its resources.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.__enter__
documentation: Enter nvim session as a context manager.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.__exit__
documentation:
Exit nvim session as a context manager.

Closes the event loop.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.with_decode
documentation: Initialize a new Nvim instance.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.ui_attach
documentation:
Register as a remote UI.

After this method is called, the client will receive redraw
notifications.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.ui_detach
documentation: Unregister as a remote UI.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.ui_try_resize
documentation:
Notify nvim that the client window has resized.

If possible, nvim will send a redraw request to resize.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.subscribe
documentation: Subscribe to a Nvim event.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.unsubscribe
documentation: Unsubscribe to a Nvim event.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.command
documentation: Execute a single ex command.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.command_output
documentation: Execute a single ex command and return the output.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.eval
documentation: Evaluate a vimscript expression.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.call
documentation: Call a vimscript function.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.exec_lua
documentation:
Execute lua code.

Additional parameters are available as `...` inside the lua chunk.
Only statements are executed.  To evaluate an expression, prefix it
with `return`: `return my_function(...)`

There is a shorthand syntax to call lua functions with arguments:

    nvim.lua.func(1,2)
    nvim.lua.mymod.myfunction(data, async_=True)

is equivalent to

    nvim.exec_lua("return func(...)", 1, 2)
    nvim.exec_lua("mymod.myfunction(...)", data, async_=True)

Note that with `async_=True` there is no return value.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.strwidth
documentation:
Return the number of display cells `string` occupies.

Tab is counted as one cell.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.list_runtime_paths
documentation: Return a list of paths contained in the 'runtimepath' option.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.foreach_rtp
documentation:
Invoke `cb` for each path in 'runtimepath'.

Call the given callable for each path in 'runtimepath' until either
callable returns something but None, the exception is raised or there
are no longer paths. If stopped in case callable returned non-None,
vim.foreach_rtp function returns the value returned by callable.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.chdir
documentation: Run os.chdir, then all appropriate vim stuff.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.feedkeys
documentation: Push `keys` to Nvim user input buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.input
documentation:
Push `bytes` to Nvim low level input buffer.

Unlike `feedkeys()`, this uses the lowest level input buffer and the
call is not deferred. It returns the number of bytes actually
written(which can be less than what was requested if the buffer is
full).

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.replace_termcodes
documentation:
Replace any terminal code strings by byte sequences.

The returned sequences are Nvim's internal representation of keys,
for example:

<esc> -> '\x1b'
<cr>  -> '\r'
<c-l> -> '\x0c'
<up>  -> '\x80ku'

The returned sequences can be used as input to `feedkeys`.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.out_write
documentation:
Print `msg` as a normal message.

The message is buffered (won't display) until linefeed ("\n").

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.err_write
documentation:
Print `msg` as an error message.

The message is buffered (won't display) until linefeed ("\n").

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.quit
documentation:
Send a quit command to Nvim.

By default, the quit command is 'qa!' which will make Nvim quit without
saving anything.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.new_highlight_source
documentation: Return new src_id for use with Buffer.add_highlight.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Nvim.async_call
documentation:
Schedule `fn` to be called by the event loop soon.

This function is thread-safe, and is the only way code not
on the main thread could interact with nvim api objects.

This function can also be called in a synchronous
event handler, just before it returns, to defer execution
that shouldn't block neovim.

Class that represents a remote Nvim instance.

This class is main entry point to Nvim remote API, it is a wrapper
around Session instances.

The constructor of this class must not be called directly. Instead, the
`from_session` class method should be used to create the first instance
from a raw `Session` instance.

Subsequent instances for the same session can be created by calling the
`with_decode` instance method to change the decoding behavior or
`SubClass.from_nvim(nvim)` where `SubClass` is a subclass of `Nvim`, which
is useful for having multiple `Nvim` objects that behave differently
without one affecting the other.

When this library is used on python3.4+, asyncio event loop is guaranteed
to be used. It is available as the "loop" attribute of this class. Note
that asyncio callbacks cannot make blocking requests, which includes
accessing state-dependent attributes. They should instead schedule another
callback using nvim.async_call, which will not have this restriction.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers.__init__
documentation: Initialize a Buffers object with Nvim object `nvim`.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers.__len__
documentation: Return the count of buffers.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers.__getitem__
documentation: Return the Buffer object matching buffer number `number`.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers.__contains__
documentation: Return whether Buffer `b` is a known valid buffer.

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Buffers.__iter__
documentation: Return an iterator over the list of buffers.

Remote NVim buffers.

Currently the interface for interacting with remote NVim buffers is the
`nvim_list_bufs` msgpack-rpc function. Most methods fetch the list of
buffers from NVim.

Conforms to *python-buffers*.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.CompatibilitySession
documentation: Helper class for API compatibility.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Current
documentation: Helper class for emulating vim.current from python-vim.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.Funcs
documentation: Helper class for functional vimscript interface.

classes.name: pynvim.api.nvim.LuaFuncs

methods.name: pynvim.api.nvim.LuaFuncs.__getattr__
documentation: Return wrapper to named api method.
Wrapper to allow lua functions to be called like python methods.
